elements.text: B2-3-02, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Factory-Built Housing (02/04/2026)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on factory-built housing, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Modular, Prefabricated, Panelized, or Sectional Housing Eligibility
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Modular, Prefabricated, Panelized, or Sectional Housing Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Modular Construction Techniques on Multi-Unit Buildings
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae defines a 'manufactured home' as any dwelling unit built on a permanent chassis that is attached to a permanent foundation system and evidenced by a HUD Data Plate and HUD Certification Label(s). For additional information, see B5-2-02, Manufactured Housing Loan Eligibility. (The terms 'manufactured home' and 'manufactured housing' are used interchangeably in this Guide).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The table below provides additional manufactured housing property eligibility requirements. For manufactured housing appraisal requirements, see B4-1.4-01, Factory-Built Housing: Manufactured Housing. For project review methods for manufactured homes located in a condo or PUD, see B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The manufactured home must be built in compliance with
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the Federal Manufactured Home Construction and Safety Standards that were established
elements.label: text

elements.text: June 15, 1976, as amended and in force at the time the home is manufactured; and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • additional requirements that appear in HUD regulations at 24 C.F.R. Part 3280.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Compliance with these standards will be evidenced by the presence of either a HUD Data Plate
elements.label: text

elements.text: or the HUD Certification Label(s) for each section of the home for existing construction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must obtain photos of the original HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s) or
elements.label: text

elements.text: provide the alternative documentation described below.  If neither of these can be obtained,
elements.label: text

elements.text: the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. (Both are required for new construction).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The HUD Data Plate is a paper document located on the interior of the subject property that contains, among other things, the manufacturer's name and trade/model number. In addition to the data required by Fannie Mae, the Data Plate includes pertinent information about the unit, including a list of factory-installed equipment. The HUD Certification Label(s), sometimes referred to as a HUD 'seal' or 'tag,' is a metal plate located on the exterior of each section of the home. The Manufactured Home Appraisal Report (Form 1004C) must have photos of either the HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certification Label(s).
elements.label: text

elements.text: As an alternative to the original HUD Certification Label(s) or the HUD Data Plate for existing manufactured homes, the lender must obtain either a label verification letter with the same information contained on the HUD Certification Label(s) or duplicate HUD Data Plate from the Institute for Building Technology and Safety (IBTS). A duplicate HUD Data Plate may also be available by contacting the In-Plant Primary Inspection Agency (IPIA) or the manufacturer. (A list of IPIA offices is posted on HUD's website.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: The unit must not have been previously installed or occupied at any other site or location, except from the manufacturer or the dealer's lot as a new unit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The manufactured home must be a one-unit dwelling that is legally classified as real property and cannot include an accessory dwelling unit. See B2-3-04, Special Property Eligibility Considerations for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The towing hitch, wheels, and axles must be removed. The dwelling must assume the characteristics of site-built housing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The manufactured home must be at least 12 feet wide and have a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade finished area.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Except for MH Advantage properties, Fannie Mae does not specify other minimum requirements for size, roof pitch, or any other specific construction details for HUD-coded manufactured homes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Site preparation for delivery of the manufactured home must be completed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The manufactured home must be attached to a permanent foundation system in accordance with the manufacturer's requirements for anchoring, support, stability, and maintenance. The foundation system must be appropriate for the soil conditions for the site and meet local and state codes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The manufactured home must be permanently connected to a septic tank or sewage system, and to other utilities in accordance with local and state requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the property is not situated on a publicly dedicated and maintained street, then it must be situated on a street that is community owned and maintained, or privately owned and maintained.
elements.label: text

elements.text: There must be adequate vehicular access and there must be an adequate and legally enforceable agreement for vehicular access and maintenance. See B4-1.3-04, Site Section of the Appraisal Report, for additional information about privately maintained streets.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loans secured by existing manufactured homes that have incomplete items, such as a partially completed addition or renovation, or defects or needed repairs that affect safety, soundness, or structural integrity, are not eligible for purchase until the necessary work is completed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions to the foregoing may be made only for minor items that do not affect the ability to obtain an occupancy permit - such as landscaping, a driveway, or a walkway - subject to all requirements and warranties for new or proposed construction provided in B4-1.2-05,
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for Verifying Completion and Postponed Improvements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Manufactured homes that have an addition or have had a structural modification are eligible under certain conditions. If the state in which the property is located requires inspection by a state agency to approve modifications to the property, then the lender is required to confirm that the property has met the requirement. However, if the state does not have this requirement, then the structural modification must be inspected and be deemed structurally sound by a third party who is regulated by the state and is qualified to make the determination. In all cases, the satisfactory inspection report must be retained in the mortgage loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MH Advantage is manufactured housing that is built to meet construction, architectural design, and energy efficiency standards that are more consistent with site-built homes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples of the physical characteristics for MH Advantage include
elements.label: text

elements.text: specific architectural and aesthetic features such as distinctive roof treatments (eaves and higher pitch roofline), masonry or poured concrete perimeter foundation, attached garage or carport, covered porch;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: construction elements including durability features, such as siding and cabinetry materials; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: energy efficiency standards (minimum energy ratings apply).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae has agreements with manufacturers of homes intended to qualify for MH Advantage based on specific design criteria. The agreement may allow the manufacturers to apply an MH Advantage Sticker to single- or multi-width manufactured homes that meet the design criteria. With the exception of the lender requirements outlined below, lenders will not be required to independently determine the property's eligibility for MH Advantage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders' and appraisers' responsibilities relating to MH Advantage loans are detailed below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: ensure the property meets the MH Advantage eligibility requirements by reviewing appraisal photos that show the MH Advantage Sticker or the CHOICEHome® label (placed in proximity to the HUD Data Plate),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure that for existing construction either the HUD Data Plate or the HUD
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure for new construction, both the HUD Data Plate and the HUD Certification Labels are present.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: verify through the appraisal, Appraisal Update and/or Completion Report (Form 1004D), completion alternatives, or photos that the property includes the following site and access features:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Meet HUD's Permanent Foundation Guide for Manufactured Housing
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Engineered Foundation certified by a registered architect or professional engineer; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Be a low-profile/residential set finished floor set
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Not exceed 30 inches from the bottom of the first-floor joist to the exterior grade for the front or entry
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Have a covered porch (minimum 72 sq. ft.)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For a single-section MH Advantage or CHOICEHome with a date of manufacture after June 4, 2026, an attached garage or carport for new construction with space to accommodate at least one car. The exterior materials and finishes for the attached garage must be at least equivalent to the remainder of the structure.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ Must lead to the home (or to the garage/carport if present).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ Material must be blacktop, pavers, bricks, concrete, or cement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ Must connect either the driveway or an attached garage/carport to the front entrance of the home.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ Material must be blacktop, pavers, flagstone, bricks, concrete, or cement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ Verify that the property includes a gutter and downspout system to direct water away from the foundation and structure.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If an existing infill lot for a new construction cannot accommodate an attached garage/carport, driveway, and/or sidewalk, the feature and/or site improvement is not required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: An infill lot is vacant or underutilized land located within a developed urban and suburban area, typically surrounded by existing buildings and infrastructure. It specifically excludes any lot located in a newly created or recently platted subdivisions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For new construction, the lender is responsible for compliance with Fannie Mae's standard appraisal requirements, specifically B4-1.2-05, Requirements for Verifying Completion and Postponed Improvements and, B4-1.4-01, Factory-Built Housing: Manufactured Housing for appraisals based on plans and specifications. This will ensure that site improvements that are not attached to the home, such as detached garages, are complete.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standard valuation requirements for manufactured homes apply, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: B4-1.3-05, Improvements Section of the Appraisal Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-1.3-10, Cost and Income Approach to Value
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Manufactured Home Appraisal Report (1004C), Appraisal Update and/or Completion Report (Form 1004D), or completion alternatives must include photos of the MH Advantage Sticker or the CHOICEHome® label, as applicable, and either the HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certification Label(s), and the site showing all driveways, sidewalks, and detached structures located on the site.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The requirement of photos of either the HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certification Label(s) applies to existing construction. (Both are required for new construction.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unless stated otherwise, loans secured by manufactured homes that meet the MH Advantage criteria are subject to the same requirements that apply to all manufactured homes. (References to 'manufactured homes' or 'manufactured housing' apply to MH Advantage unless an exception is stated.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note that loans secured by MH Advantage properties are afforded a number of flexibilities over standard manufactured housing, including higher LTV ratios, standard mortgage insurance, and reduced loan-level price adjustments. See the Eligibility Matrix ,  B7-1-02, Mortgage Insurance Coverage Requirements, and Loan-Level Price Adjustment Matrix , respectively, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Modular, Prefabricated, Panelized, or Sectional Housing Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Modular Homes . Fannie Mae purchases loans secured by modular homes built in accordance with the International Residential Code administered by state agencies responsible for adopting and administering building code requirements for the state in which the modular home is installed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Prefabricated, Panelized, and Sectional Homes. Loans secured by prefabricated, panelized, or sectional housing are eligible for purchase. These properties do not have to satisfy HUD's Federal Manufactured Home Construction and Safety Standards or the International Residential Codes that are adopted and administered by the state in which the home is installed. The home must conform to local building codes in the area in which it will be located.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Modular, Prefabricated, Panelized, or Sectional Housing Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Factory-built housing such as modular, prefabricated, panelized, or sectional housing is not considered manufactured housing and is eligible under the guidelines for one-unit properties. These types of properties must
elements.label: text

elements.text: be built of the same quality of materials as and assume the characteristics of site-built housing,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: be legally classified as real property, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: conform to all local building codes in the jurisdiction in which they are permanently located.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The purchase, conveyance, and financing (or refinancing) must be evidenced by a valid and enforceable first- lien mortgage or deed of trust that is recorded in the land records, and must represent a single real estate transaction under applicable state law. The lender is responsible for perfecting the real estate title and obtaining any needed title endorsements before selling the loan to Fannie Mae when a unit is titled as personal property similarly to manufactured homes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All factory-built units must be permanently attached to a foundation that meets the standards for local building codes where the unit will be placed and in accordance with the recommendations prescribed by the unit's manufacturer (when applicable). If the unit had axles, wheels, tow hitch, or other hardware to facilitate ease of transportation to the site, the lender is responsible for ensuring that all such hardware is removed prior to selling the loan to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae affords modular, prefabricated, panelized, or sectional housing homes the same treatment as sitebuilt housing. Therefore, Fannie Mae does not have minimum requirements for width, size, roof pitch, or any other specific construction details.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Modular Construction Techniques on Multi-Unit Buildings
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Multi-unit buildings such as condos, co-ops, and townhomes may be constructed, in whole or in part, through the use of modular construction techniques. All buildings must conform to local building codes in the jurisdiction in which they are permanently located. Units in these buildings are provided the same treatment as units in multi-unit buildings constructed with site-built techniques.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B2-3-03, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Leasehold Estates (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on leasehold estates, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrower's Option to Purchase Fee Interest
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that are secured by properties subject to leasehold estates in areas in which this type of property ownership has received market acceptance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: manufactured homes located in a condo or PUD project approved by Fannie Mae's Project Review Eligibility Service (PERS). See  B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A loan secured by a unit in a project that is subject to a ground lease is considered a loan secured by a leasehold estate for purposes of this topic. For these loans, lenders must also comply with all legal and eligibility requirements for condos, co-ops, and PUDs. See Chapter B4-2, Project Standards, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following definitions apply to this topic:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A loan secured by a leasehold estate is also known as a "leasehold mortgage."
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The term "lease" includes any form of lease or ground lease (for example, a master lease, business lease, sublease, or unit lease), together with any addendum, amendment, or rider, or a memorandum thereof.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The terms "lessor" and "lessee" includes sublessor and sublessee.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For loans secured by units in projects subject to a ground lease:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower may also be called the "unit lessee," "unit mortgagor," or "unit owner."
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The "ground lease" may also be called a "land lease" or "underlying lease."
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For co-ops, this topic does not apply to a borrower's "proprietary lease" or "occupancy agreement."
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For a loan secured by a unit in a project, the term "lender" does not refer to the project's blanket lender or blanket mortgagee, if applicable.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must comply with all requirements for leasehold mortgages. In addition, the lender agrees that in accordance with A2-2-07, Life-of-Loan Representations and Warranties, any failure to comply at any time with requirements in this topic is a breach of the life-of-loan representations and warranties if it impacts first-lien enforceability.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table provides general requirements for leasehold mortgages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The loan must be secured by a first lien in the property improvements and the borrower's
elements.label: text

elements.text: rights in the leasehold interest in the land.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For co-op share loans, see  B4-2.3-03, Legal Requirements for Co-op Projects  and  B4-2.3-04,
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan Eligibility for Co-op Share Loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: : For loans sold to Fannie Mae with new leases entered into on or after Sept. 01, 2025:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The fee estate must not be subject to any prior secured loans or other liens, unless the
elements.label: text

elements.text: secured party or lienholder has agreed to recognize and not disturb the lease if it becomes the
elements.label: text

elements.text: owner of the fee estate, as evidenced by an agreement recorded in the appropriate land
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease estate and the improvements must constitute real property and be subject to the
elements.label: text

elements.text: For co-op share loans, see  B4-2.3-03, Legal Requirements for Co-op Projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The appraisal must meet the requirements in  B4-1.4-05, Leasehold Interests Appraisal Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The loan must meet the applicable title insurance requirements in  B7-2-04, Special Title Insurance Coverage Considerations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All rents, other payments, or assessments under the lease that have become due must be paid.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease must not be in default under any provision of the lease and the lessor must not have claimed any such default.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease must be recorded in the appropriate land records.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease must be in full force and effect, and enforceable in all respects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must ensure all leases associated with the subject property, regardless of the form of the lease (including a master lease, sublease, or unit lease) comply with the requirements below. The lessee (or sublessee, when applicable) must be the borrower, condo or PUD homeowners' association (HOA) or the co-op corporation. For manufactured homes located in a condo or PUD project approved by PERS, the HOA must be the lessee without any further sublessees.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Compliance with these lease requirements may be satisfied by:
elements.label: text

elements.text: separate agreement(s) incorporated into the lease (e.g., addendum, amendment, or rider), or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project's constituent documents (project documents) (e.g., for co-ops, the Recognition Agreement or other agreement).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : For units in projects subject to a ground lease in which the HOA or co-op corporation is the lessee, the lender must ensure the lease complies with these lease requirements unless the project has been approved by Fannie Mae in Condo Project Manager (CPM).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Regardless of the lessee, the lender must ensure the terms of the lease address all of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease must have an unexpired term that exceeds the maturity date of the loan by five (5) years or more.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lease must not preclude the borrower's membership or voting rights in the HOA or co-op corporation, as applicable.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the loan is secured by a sublease, a default under the master lease will not automatically result in the termination of the sublease.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following table provides additional lease requirements depending on whether the borrower or the HOA or co-op corporation is the lessee.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lease Requirements if the Borrower is the Lessee
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lease Requirements if the HOA or Co-op Corporation is the Lessee
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lease must allow the lease for it (including the lessee's option to purchase) to be assigned, transferred, mortgaged, and subleased an unlimited number of times either without restriction or on payment of a reasonable fee and delivery of reasonable documentation to the lessor. The lease must not require a credit review or impose other qualifying criteria on any assignee, transferee, mortgagee, or sublessee.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lease may, but is not required to, include an option for the borrower to purchase the fee interest in the land. Both the lease and the option to purchase must be assignable, together, subject to permissible restrictions noted above.

elements.text: An ADU is typically an additional living area independent of the primary dwelling that may have been added to, created within, or detached from a primary one-unit dwelling. The ADU must provide for living, sleeping, cooking, and bathroom facilities and be on the same parcel as the primary one-unit dwelling.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table describes the requirements for classifying an ADU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: • Only one ADU is permitted on the parcel of the primary one-unit dwelling.
elements.label: text

elements.text: • ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling.
elements.label: text

elements.text: ⚬ be subordinate in size to the primary dwelling.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ have the following separate features from the primary dwelling:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The ADU may, but is not required to, include access to the primary dwelling. However, it is not considered an ADU if it can only be accessed through the primary dwelling or the area is open to the primary dwelling with no expectation of privacy.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The kitchen must, at a minimum, contain the following:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ a sink with running water; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ a stove or stove hookup (hotplates, microwaves, or toaster ovens are not acceptable stove substitutes).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: An independent second kitchen by itself does not constitute an ADU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The removal of a stove does not change the ADU classification.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See B3-3.8-01, Rental Income for the requirements that need to be satisfied in order to include rental income in qualifying.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The construction method of an ADU can be site- or factory-built, including modular, and single- or multi-width HUD Code manufactured homes that are legally classified as real property. If an ADU is present, the primary dwelling must be site-built or a modular home. If the ADU is a HUD Code manufactured home, the lender must verify the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the property was built in compliance with the Federal Manufactured Home Construction and Safety Standards (established June 15, 1976, as amended and in force at the time the home was manufactured),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: it is attached to a permanent foundation system in accordance with the manufacturer's requirements for anchoring, support, stability, and maintenance,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the foundation system must be appropriate for the soil conditions for the site and meet local and state codes,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: it is encumbered by the mortgage with the primary dwelling, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: additional requirements that appear in HUD regulations in 24 C.F.R. Part 3280.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Compliance with these standards must be evidenced by photos of the HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s) (for each section of the home) in the appraisal. If the original or alternative documentation cannot be
elements.label: text

elements.text: obtained for the HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certification Label(s), the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. See B2-3-02, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Factory-Built Housing, for more information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples of ADUs include, (but are not limited to):
elements.label: text

elements.text: a living area over a garage,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a living area in a basement,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a small addition to the primary dwelling, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a manufactured home (legally classified as real property).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Whether a property is defined as a one-unit property with an ADU or a two- to four-unit property will be based on the characteristics of the property, which may include, but are not limited to, the existence of separate utility meter(s), a unique postal address, and whether the unit can be legally rented. The appraiser must determine compliance with this definition as part of the analysis in the Highest and Best Use section of the appraisal. See B4-1.3-05, Improvements Section of the Appraisal Report for additional ADU appraisal requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Some ADUs may predate the adoption of the local zoning ordinance and therefore be classified as legal nonconforming. An ADU should always be considered legal if it is allowed under the current zoning code for the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The ownership and debt financing structures commonly found with solar panels are key to determining whether the panels are third-party owned, personal property of the homeowner, or a fixture to the real estate. Common ownership or financing structures include:

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae will purchase or securitize a mortgage loan on a property with solar panels. If the borrower is, or will be, the owner of the solar panels (meaning the panels were a cash purchase, were included in the home purchase price, were otherwise financed and repaid in full, or are secured by the existing first mortgage), our standard requirements apply (for example, appraisal, insurance, and title).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Properties with solar panels and other energy efficient items financed with a PACE loan are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if the PACE loan is not paid in full prior to or at closing. For additional information, see B5-3.4-01, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans.

elements.text: Lenders are responsible for determining the ownership and any financing structure of the subject property's solar panels in order to properly underwrite the loan and maintain first lien position of the mortgage. When financing is involved, lenders may be able to make this determination by evaluating the borrower's credit report for solar-related debt and by asking the borrower for a copy of all related documentation for the loan. The lender must also review the title report to determine if the related debt is reflected in the land records associated with the subject property. If insufficient documentation is available and the ownership status of the panels is unclear, no value for the panels may be attributed to the property value on the appraisal unless the lender obtains a Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) 'personal property' search that confirms the solar panels are not claimed as collateral by any non-mortgage lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: A UCC financing statement that covers personal property and is not intended as a 'fixture filing' must be filed in the office identified in the relevant state's adopted version of the UCC.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders are responsible for ensuring the appraiser has accurate information about the ownership structure of the solar panels and that the appraisal appropriately addresses any impact to the property's value. Separately financed solar panels must not contribute to the value of the property unless the related documents indicate the panels cannot be repossessed in the event of default on the associated financing. Any contributory value for owned or financed solar panels must comply with Energy Efficiency Improvements in  B4-1.3-05, Improvements Section of the Appraisal Report .

elements.text: *A fixture filing is a UCC-1 financing statement authorized and made in accordance with the UCC adopted in the state in which the related real property is located. It covers property that is, or will be, affixed to improvements to such real property. It contains both a description of the collateral that is, or is to be, affixed to that such property, and a description of such real property. It is filed in the same office that mortgages are recorded under the law of the state in which the real property is located. Filing in the land records provides notice to third parties, including title insurance companies, of the existence and perfection of a security interest in the fixture. If properly filed, the security interest in the described fixture has priority over the lien of a subsequently recorded mortgage.

elements.text: If the solar panels are leased from or owned by a third party under a power purchase agreement or other similar lease arrangement, the following requirements apply (whether to the original agreement or as subsequently amended).

elements.text: Lender Requirements for Properties with Solar Panels that are Leased or Covered by a Power Purchase Agreement
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must obtain and review copies of the lease or power purchase agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The monthly lease payment must be included in the DTI ratio calculation unless the lease is structured to
elements.label: text

elements.text: provide delivery of a specific amount of energy at a fixed payment during a given period, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: • have a production guarantee that compensates the borrower on a prorated basis in the
elements.label: text

elements.text: event the solar panels fail to meet the energy output required for in the lease for that period.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Payments under power purchase agreements where the payment is calculated solely based on
elements.label: text

elements.text: the energy produced may be excluded from the DTI ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The value of the solar panels cannot be included in the appraised value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The value of the solar panels must not be included in the LTV ratio calculation, even if a precautionary UCC filing is recorded because the documented lease or power purchase agreement status takes priority.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: A 'precautionary' UCC filing is one that lessors often file to put third parties on notice of their claimed ownership interest in the property described in it. When the only property described in the UCC filing as collateral is the solar equipment covered by the lease or power purchase agreement, and not the home or underlying land, such a precautionary UCC filing is acceptable (and a minor impediment to title), as long as the loan is underwritten in accordance with this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The value of the solar panels must not be included in other debt secured by real estate in the CLTV ratio calculation because the documented lease or power purchase agreement status takes priority.

elements.text: B2-3-05, Properties Affected by a Disaster (09/03/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on properties affected by a disaster, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Value Acceptance Offers Following a Disaster
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Mortgage Selling and Servicing Contract requires the lender to warrant for each loan it delivers to Fannie Mae that the property is not damaged by fire, wind, or other cause of loss and that there are no proceedings pending for the partial or total condemnation of the property. The lender also warrants that the loan conforms to all applicable requirements in the Selling Guide , including the requirement that the loan is an acceptable investment. Finally, the lender represents and warrants that it knows of nothing involving the loan or the property that can reasonably be expected to cause the loan to become delinquent or adversely affect the mortgage's value or marketability.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must be able to make the warranties that are described above. Therefore, before delivery of a loan to Fannie Mae where the property may have been damaged by a disaster, the lender is expected to take prudent and reasonable actions to determine whether the condition of the property may have materially changed. The lender is responsible for determining if an inspection of the property and/or new appraisal is necessary to support this warranty. If a property is located in a condo or co-op project, both the condition of the unit and the condition of the building in which the unit is located must be assessed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders should use the following criteria when determining if the loan can be delivered to Fannie Mae:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the property has been damaged and the damage does not affect the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property and the repair items are covered by insurance, the lender may deliver the loan to Fannie Mae. In these circumstances, the lender must obtain documentation of the professional estimates of the repair costs and must ensure that sufficient funds are available for the borrower's benefit to guarantee the completion of the repairs.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the property was damaged and the damage is uninsured or the damage affects the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property, the property must be repaired before the loan is delivered to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: These requirements are necessary to support the lender's property representations and warranties and apply through the end of the delivery process which is the whole loan purchase date or MBS settlement date. For DU loan casefiles with a value acceptance or value acceptance + property data offer, the lender may exercise the offer as long as they have complied with the above requirements with regard to property condition and repairs. This applies in addition to the value acceptance requirements in B4-1.4-10, Value Acceptance and B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Data.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The above requirements do not apply to high LTV refinance loans. See B5-7-02, High LTV Refinance Underwriting, Documentation, and Collateral Requirements for the New Loan for additional information. For delivered loan requirements, see the Servicing Guide , Chapter D1-3.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU is updated periodically to incorporate ZIP codes included in FEMA-Declared Disaster Areas eligible for Individual Assistance. Fannie Mae may also add areas impacted by other disasters or emergencies at its discretion. New loan casefiles for properties in those ZIP codes are excluded from consideration for a new value acceptance or value acceptance + property data offer.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Value Acceptance Offers Following a Disaster
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: After Fannie Mae has received an acceptable appraisal that was performed following a disaster, that appraisal can serve as the basis for a future value acceptance or value acceptance + property data offer. Lenders may exercise these offers in accordance with the requirements in B4-1.4-10, Value Acceptance and B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Data.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a loan is secured by a property located in a FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for individual assistance, Fannie Mae provides additional flexibilities. The following requirements apply:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The underwriting documentation, including credit reports and verifications of income and assets, must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders may disregard the message in the DU Underwriting Findings Report that indicates if the
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loan casefiles has not already closed, the credit report has expired.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The appraisal must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date. Lenders must comply with the property eligibility requirements above.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders who wish to receive representation and warranty relief offered by the DU validation service must continue to comply with all conditions in the DU Underwriting Findings Report, including the close by date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loans originated in accordance with the age of documentation flexibilities, must be delivered to Fannie Mae no later than two years from the date of the disaster declaration by FEMA.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See B5-4.2-02, Disaster-Related Limited Cash-Out Refinance Flexibilities for information related to certain flexibilities offered for a disaster related limited cash-out transaction.

elements.text: This subpart contains borrower underwriting policies for conventional mortgage loans that are sold to Fannie Mae. Fannie Mae offers lenders two options for conducting a comprehensive risk assessment-automated underwriting through DU or manual underwriting. Both methods include an evaluation of the borrower's equity investment, credit history, liquid reserves, reliable and recurring income, and the cumulative effect that these and other risk factors have on mortgage loan performance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter provides lenders with information on the comprehensive risk assessment approach to manual underwriting.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains information on the comprehensive risk assessment approach to underwriting, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae's underwriting policies include an evaluation of the borrower's (or spouse's to the extent required by applicable law) equity investment, credit history, liquid reserves, reliable and recurring income, and the cumulative effect that these and other risk factors have on mortgage loan performance. Fannie Mae's underwriting policies enable the lender to consider various scenarios in evaluating a borrower's willingness and capacity to repay the mortgage loan. The lender must confirm that information provided by the borrower during the loan application process is accurate and complete; include documentation in the loan file that supports the lender's assessment of the borrower's credit history, employment and income, assets, and other financial information used for qualifying; conduct a comprehensive risk assessment of each mortgage loan application; and render a decision to either approve or decline the mortgage loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae offers lenders two options for conducting a comprehensive risk assessment-automated underwriting through DU or manual underwriting. Both methods include an evaluation of the borrower's equity investment, credit history, liquid reserves, reliable and recurring income, and the cumulative effect that these and other risk factors have on mortgage loan performance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders that choose to manually underwrite a mortgage application are expected to follow the comprehensive risk assessment approach. Under this approach, lenders evaluate certain key risk elements to assess the overall level of delinquency risk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: evaluating the delinquency risk of each loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: reviewing the credit report, as well as all other credit information, to determine that the credit report meets Fannie Mae's requirements, that the data evaluated was accurate, and that the borrower has the capacity to repay the mortgage loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: assessing the adequacy of the property as collateral for the mortgage requested;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: determining whether or not the loan meets Fannie Mae's eligibility requirements as fully described in this Guide;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: determining whether or not it is appropriate to deliver the mortgage loan to Fannie Mae; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: fully documenting the assessment and the documentation on which the assessment was based.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders must evaluate the overall level of serious delinquency risk that is present in each mortgage application by taking into consideration any layering of risk factors, the significance of risk factors, and the overall risks present in the mortgage application. The Eligibility Matrix provides a solid foundation for assessing the risk of a manually underwritten loan and identifies the risk elements to evaluate for each transaction type.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender's determination of the mortgage delinquency risk, the assessment of the adequacy of the property as security for the mortgage, the determination of whether the mortgage satisfies Fannie Mae's mortgage eligibility criteria, and the acceptability of the documentation in the mortgage file should all enter into the decision on whether to deliver the mortgage to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must fully document the results of its comprehensive risk assessment and final underwriting decision and ensure that the information used to reach its comprehensive risk assessment is valid, accurate, and substantiated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For a more precise or definitive recommendation for determining whether to deliver a given mortgage to Fannie Mae, the lender should submit the mortgage application to DU. DU evaluates the probability of future serious delinquency and arrives at an underwriting recommendation by relying on a comprehensive examination of risk factors in a mortgage application. Furthermore, DU is the standard by which Fannie Mae assesses the delinquency risk on all mortgages sold to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The table below provides references to recently issued Announcements that are related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Introduction This chapter describes DU considerations and requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains general information on DU, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae's underwriting policies include an evaluation of the borrower's (or spouse's to the extent required by applicable law) equity investment, credit history, liquid reserves, reliable and recurring income, and the cumulative effect that these and other risk factors have on mortgage loan performance. Fannie Mae's underwriting policies enable the lender to consider various scenarios in evaluating a borrower's willingness and capacity to repay the mortgage loan. The lender must confirm that information provided by the borrower during the loan application process is accurate and complete; include documentation in the loan file that supports the lender's assessment of the borrower's credit history, employment and income, assets, and other financial information used for qualifying; conduct a comprehensive risk assessment of each mortgage loan application; and render a decision to either approve or decline the mortgage loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae offers lenders two options for conducting a comprehensive risk assessment-automated underwriting through DU or manual underwriting. Both methods include an evaluation of the borrower's equity investment, credit history, liquid reserves, reliable and recurring income, and the cumulative effect that these and other risk factors have on mortgage loan performance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU evaluates mortgage delinquency risk and arrives at an underwriting recommendation by relying on a comprehensive examination of the primary and contributory risk factors in a mortgage application. (See B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU) It analyzes the information in the loan casefile to reach an overall credit risk assessment to determine eligibility for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: No one factor determines a borrower's ability or willingness to make their mortgage payments. DU identifies low-risk factors that can offset high-risk factors. When several high-risk factors are present in a loan casefile without sufficient offsets, the likelihood of serious delinquency increases.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU conducts its analysis uniformly, and without regard to race, gender, or other prohibited factors. DU uses validated, statistically significant variables that have been shown to be predictive of mortgage delinquency across all groups.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU does not evaluate a loan's compliance with federal and state laws and regulations including, without limitation, a loan's potential status as a qualified mortgage under applicable laws and regulations. Lenders bear sole responsibility for complying with applicable laws and regulations, and these compliance obligations may not be imposed upon or shared by Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must enter loan data (including data from the Form 1003) in their loan origination system or in the DU user interface before submitting for underwriting. A credit report must also be obtained. Loans may be submitted to DU before or after the closing of the mortgage loan; however, the first submission to DU for underwriting purposes must occur before closing of the mortgage loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the mortgage loan or borrower information changes and it no longer matches the information used when the loan casefile was last underwritten with DU, the lender must update the data and resubmit the loan casefile to DU. Exceptions are specified in B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU after closing and prior to delivery to Fannie Mae, the lender is responsible for ensuring that:
elements.label: text

elements.text: all information provided in the final submission to DU matches the terms of the closed loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan complies with the requirements specified in A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan delivery data matches both the closed loan and the final data submitted to DU; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan casefile receives an eligible recommendation from DU on the final submission.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender may request a new credit report after closing when the loan casefile is resubmitted and, as with all loan casefiles, must comply with the Fair Credit Reporting Act with regard to the purpose and nature of the inquiry. If the new credit report contains information that is different than the information used to prepare the final loan application that was signed by the borrower at closing, the loan application must be updated. (Borrower signature(s) are not required due to the update occurring post-closing.) The lender must include both the final signed and the updated unsigned loan applications in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The credit report must meet the allowable age of documents as of the note date. If the credit report expired prior to the note date and the loan casefile is being resubmitted to DU, a new credit report must be requested.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In certain instances, the lender may not be able to access the original DU loan casefile for resubmission purposes. Lenders may create a new loan casefile in DU after closing to ensure that all information in the final DU submission matches the terms of the closed loan, provided all of the following conditions are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the above lender responsibilities are met, including the updating of the final loan application, if applicable;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan has not yet been delivered to Fannie Mae;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan has the same information (for example, the same borrower(s) and property) as had previously been underwritten through DU prior to closing using another loan casefile, and that loan casefile received an eligible recommendation from DU;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender retains the DU Underwriting Findings Report from the original loan casefile ID in the loan file;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the DU submission using the new loan casefile occurs no more than 60 days after closing (based on the note date) or 18 months after initial closing for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans (described
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: in B5-3.1-02, Conversion of Construction-to-Permanent Financing: Single-Closing Transactions); and
elements.label: text

elements.text: as stated above, when a new credit report is requested, the lender complies with the Fair Credit Reporting Act.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the resubmission to DU results in an 'ineligible' recommendation, the mortgage loan may not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: If the quality control function is performed before delivery, the above requirements apply. If quality control is performed after delivery, see Chapter D1-3, Lender Post-Closing QC Mortgage Review.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU issues two types of reports:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU Underwriting Findings report summarizes the overall underwriting recommendation and lists the steps necessary for the lender to complete the processing of the loan file. This is typically the first report viewed by an underwriter or a loan officer after the loan casefile has been underwritten with DU. This report is described in B3-2-11, DU Underwriting Findings Report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Underwriting Analysis report contains much of the same information requested on the Uniform Underwriting and Transmittal Summary ( Form 1008).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Each time a loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the information in these reports is updated with information from the most recent submission. The date and time of each submission are recorded on each report, along with the unique loan casefile ID.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU loan casefiles are archived and no longer retained in DU from the earlier of
elements.label: text

elements.text: 270 days from the date on which the loan casefile was last updated, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 540 days from the date on which the loan casefile was created.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan casefiles for single-closing construction-to-permanent transactions (identified in DU as a Construction-toPermanent Closing Type of Single-Closing), will be archived using 660 days from the date on which the loan casefile was created.
elements.label: text

elements.text: These time frames are intended to ensure that the total volume of loans in the system is at a manageable level, reducing the time required by DU to search for and retrieve loan casefiles
elements.label: text

elements.text: After a loan casefile is archived from DU, it cannot be restored. If a loan casefile that has been archived must be re-underwritten, a new loan casefile must be created and submitted to DU. The loan casefile will be subject to the policies in effect for the current version of DU. Fannie Mae is not responsible for retaining loan casefiles for the lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following topics describe the underwriting recommendations returned by DU:
elements.label: text

elements.text: When underwriting loans with DU, the lender must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: employ prudent underwriting judgment in assessing whether a loan casefile should be approved and delivered to Fannie Mae;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirm the accuracy of the data it submits, making sure that it did not fail to submit any data that might have affected the DU recommendation had it been known;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure that the loan complies with all of the verification messages and approval conditions specified in the DU Underwriting Findings report;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: apply due diligence when reviewing the documentation in the loan file;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: review the credit report to confirm that the data that DU evaluated with respect to the borrower's credit history was accurate and complete;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: determine if there is any potentially derogatory or contradictory information that is not part of the data analyzed by DU; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: take action when erroneous data in the credit report or contradictory or derogatory information in the loan file would justify additional investigation or would provide grounds for a decision that is different from the recommendation that DU delivered.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For example, if a foreclosure was reported in the credit report but was not detected by DU (that is, it was not referenced in any verification messages), the lender must determine if the loan complies with the applicable guidelines (see B3-5.3-07, Significant Derogatory Credit Events - Waiting Periods and Re-establishing Credit).
elements.label: text

elements.text: See the Desktop Underwriter & Desktop Originator Learning Center for a number of helpful resources.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains information on the DU validation service, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Verbal Verification of Employment Alternative for Employment Validated using an Asset Verification Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The DU validation service offers lenders an opportunity to deliver loans with more certainty. Certain components of the loan file - income, employment, and assets - are eligible for validation by DU using electronic verification reports obtained from vendors. When a component of the loan is validated by DU, the loan may be eligible for representation and warranty enforcement relief related to that component. Different lender quality control and documentation requirements may also apply. See the following for additional information:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents and Federal Income Tax Returns,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-3.2-01, Standards for Employment and Income Documentation,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-3.1-02, Tax Return and Transcript Documentation Requirements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Section B3-3.4, Other Sources of Income ,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.2-01, Verification of Deposits and Assets,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-4.4-02, Requirements for Certain Assets in DU,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: D1-2-01, Lender Prefunding Quality Control Review Process, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: D1-3-02, Lender Post-Closing Quality Control Review of Approval Conditions, Underwriting Decisions, Data, and Documentation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The DU validation service is an optional service available only for conventional loans underwritten through DU. Lenders are not required to participate in the DU validation service in order for a loan to be underwritten through DU and may not require a borrower to participate in the DU validation service.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A lender may obtain a verification report directly from a 'report supplier' or from a 'report distributor' as described below:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Report suppliers have entered into an agreement with Fannie Mae to participate in the DU validation service. Report suppliers generate the report and send the report data electronically to the DU validation service. This report reflects the report supplier's name and/or logo.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Report distributors have not entered into an agreement with Fannie Mae, but have an agreement with an eligible report supplier. The report supplier (not the distributor) sends the report data electronically to the DU validation service. The verification report reflects both the report distributor's name and the name and/or logo of the applicable report supplier.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The DU Validation Service Verification Report Vendors list provides a listing of authorized report suppliers and report distributors and is available on Fannie Mae's website.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Asset verification reports provided by third-party vendors may include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: data covering up to 12 months (or more) of asset account data;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a report showing a subset of information typically found on an asset verification report (for example, deposit transaction information with no corresponding amount or account balance) intended to be used to verify employment using direct deposit transactions (a "supplemental asset report").
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: To validate income or employment using an asset verification report, the lender must order an asset verification report with a minimum of 12 months of data from an authorized vendor.
elements.label: text

elements.text: No special approval is required from Fannie Mae to use the DU validation service, however, lenders must 'optin' to validate income and employment using an asset verification report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a lender provides the required reports, all loans that are submitted to DU will automatically be assessed through the DU validation service for validation of the related component.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To participate in the DU validation service, the lender must
elements.label: text

elements.text: have a relationship with, and have entered into a contract for the services provided by, a vendor(s) either a report supplier or a report distributor - that is authorized to obtain a verification report;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: have an agreement with a vendor(s) that
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: allows for the report supplier to share the information contained within the verification report (obtained by the lender) with Fannie Mae electronically for use by the DU validation service; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensures the lender receives, or has access to, the same data that is shared with Fannie Mae electronically in accordance with A2-4.1-01, Establishing Loan Files; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: establish controls to manage and monitor the vendors in accordance with its own regulatory
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For loans assessed by the DU validation service, the lender must
elements.label: text

elements.text: obtain borrower authorization to receive the information from the vendor;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirm that the verification report matches the borrower;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure information entered by the lender in DU is properly documented;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: investigate and resolve any conflicting or contradictory information;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: retain a copy of all verification reports in the loan file (see A2-4.1-01, Establishing Loan Files for details), in addition to any other documentation required by DU; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure that the most current version of the verification report is used by the DU validation service. If the lender obtains an updated verification report, the lender must resubmit the loan to DU and receive a message that the component has been validated in order for the representation and warranty enforcement relief to apply.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When a component of the loan file is assessed by the DU validation service, three results are possible: validated, not validated, or unable to be validated. DU will issue a message providing the validation results.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU has determined that the information provided on the verification report supports the information entered into DU for the component being validated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU message(s) will indicate that the verification report is acceptable documentation to support the component that has been validated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU has determined that the information provided on the verification report does not fully support the information entered into DU for the component of the loan file eligible for validation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU message(s) will indicate what documentation, in addition to the verification report, is required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU is unable to validate the information entered into DU for the component eligible for validation. This could be due to DU's inability to access the verification report data, or insufficient data in the report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU message(s) will indicate what documentation is required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Regardless of the validation result, DU will continue to use the information provided by the lender in determining the DU underwriting recommendation. The results of the validation service do not override, impact, or alter any information submitted by the lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table lists the income types that can be validated, and the documentation that DU will require, which may be different than the standard documentation required in this Guide. For all eligible income types, the lender must determine the income type, amount, and source and enter this information in the applicable field in DU.

elements.text: Note: Income types not listed below are not eligible for income validation by DU.

elements.text: Note : Additional supporting documentation may be required for some income types (such as child support or alimony).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following additional information applies to income validated by DU:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If an employment and income verification report is used, the vendor must obtain income information using data obtained from the report supplier's existing database of employer-provided information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All income or direct deposit amounts shown on the income or asset verification report must be reflected in U.S. dollars.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When DU validates income, the lender
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: is not required to determine if the borrower is employed by a family member or interested parties to the property sale or purchase.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: must continue to obtain employment verification as described in  B3-3.1-04, Verbal Verification of Employment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The verification report may contain sufficient information to satisfy this requirement. See Employment-Additional Information below.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: must review the verification report relied upon by DU for validation and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: investigate and resolve any conflicting or contradictory information, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: when an asset verification report is used to validate income, the lender must confirm that the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reflects the borrower's name as account holder. The lender need only review the report the covering the period of time (30- or 60- days) required for asset verification for the transaction type, or if no asset verification is required for the transaction, the account activity covering the most recent 30-day period, for these purposes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Asset Verification Reports and Employment and Income Verification Reports: The date of the report must comply with Fannie Mae's standard age of credit document requirements as outlined in B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents and Federal Income Tax Returns.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Tax Return Transcripts: To ensure that the income validation is completed using the most recent tax transcripts, the following will be used to determine if the transcript contains the most recent tax return information. Lenders are not required to comply with the age of credit documents as outlined in B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents and Federal Income Tax Returns, when DU issues the message that income has been validated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For loan casefiles created on or before April 30, the most recent tax transcript must be provided. The most recent tax transcript would be for the prior year (current year minus 1). If the prior year tax return has not yet been filed or the transcript is not yet available, the most recent tax transcript will be the current year minus 2.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For loan casefiles created after April 30, the most recent year tax transcript must be provided for validation to be completed. The most recent tax transcript will be for the prior year (current year minus 1).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following table describes the employment that can be validated and the documentation that DU will require, which may be different than the standard documentation required in this Guide.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Employment related to the following income
elements.label: text

elements.text: Employment and Income Verification Report or
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following additional information applies to employment validated by DU:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If an employment and income verification report is used, the vendor must obtain employment information using data obtained from the report supplier's existing database of employer-provided information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Income and employment are assessed independently; however, the results of the employment validation may impact income validation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When employment is validated by DU, the validation satisfies the requirement for verbal verification of employment described in B3-3.1-04, Verbal Verification of Employment. Lenders must comply with all DU messages, including ensuring the loan closes by the 'Close by Date' stated in the DU employment validation message.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must review the verification report relied upon by DU, including any Employer Disclaimer information, if applicable, and:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: investigate and resolve any conflicting or contradictory information, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: when an asset verification report is used to validate employment, the lender must confirm that the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reflects the borrower's name as account holder. The lender need only review the report covering the period of time (30 or 60 days) required for asset verification for the transaction type, or if no asset verification is required for the transaction, the account activity covering the most recent 30-day period, for these purposes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Verbal Verification of Employment Alternative for Employment Validated using an Asset Verification Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When employment is initially validated using an asset verification report and the loan will not close by the "Close by Date" stated in the DU employment validation message, the lender may obtain a supplemental asset report from an asset verification report vendor and either submit it to DU or perform a manual review of the report to satisfy the verbal verification of employment described in  B3-3.1-04, Verbal Verification of Employment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When used to reverify employment (whether through an automated or a manual review), the supplemental asset report must contain
elements.label: text

elements.text: the account numbers and the account holder name for each account included in the report,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the date of the report, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the date and deposit details of the deposits reflected on the report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must review the report to confirm the borrower is listed as an account holder. The lender must not have any information indicating the borrower may no longer be employed and must investigate and resolve any contradictory or conflicting information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a manual review is performed, the lender must obtain the report within the same timeframe required for a verbal verification of employment and additionally, review the report to confirm
elements.label: text

elements.text: the deposit details of the direct deposits that are being used to reverify employment
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: match the ACH details identified in the DU findings messages for the direct deposit streams used by DU to validate employment, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: are consistent with the income source provided in DU;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the pattern of receipt of the identified direct deposits used to reverify employment does not reflect missed payments, and the latest expected payment prior to the date of the report is present.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : When a lender performs a manual review of the supplemental asset report to reverify employment, the enforcement relief from representations and warranties related to the employment validation through the DU validation service described in A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties ,A2-2-04, Limited Waiver and Enforcement Relief of Representations and Warranties does not apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All supplemental asset reports must be retained in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table lists the asset types that can be validated and the documentation that DU will require, which may be different than the standard documentation required in this Guide.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Assets held in the following accounts:
elements.label: text

elements.text: depending on the type of asset account and the
elements.label: text

elements.text: assessment conducted by DU for validation purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following additional information applies to asset validation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: All assets shown on the asset verification report must be in U.S. dollars and held in a U.S. or state regulated financial institution.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The account statements obtained from the vendor must cover the most recent:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 30 days of account activity for refinance transactions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 60 days of account activity for purchase transactions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The most recent quarter, if account information is reported on a quarterly basis
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must review the verification report (covering the account activity for the period of time required) and investigate and resolve any conflicting or contradictory information. The lender must also confirm that the borrower is listed as an account holder for each asset account.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The DU validation service automates the assessment of large deposits on purchase transactions required in B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts. When a large deposit needs to be documented, DU will issue a message specifying the amount of the large deposit, as well as the institution name and account number of the account that includes the large deposit. If no message is issued by DU, then no documentation of any large deposit appearing on the asset report is required when assets have been validated.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When the DU validation service validates assets on transactions with gift funds, the lender must ensure that the gift funds that appear as deposits on the asset verification report (if any) meet the requirements in B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the actual amount of funds required to complete the transaction is greater than the Funds Required to Close specified in DU, the lender must document liquid assets to cover the additional amount. See B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report for additional details on whether the loan must be resubmitted to DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For self-employed borrowers, if an eligible asset account is reflected as a business account on the verification report, the lender must perform a business cash flow analysis to confirm that the withdrawal of funds for this transaction will not have a negative impact on the business. If the lender determines the withdrawal would have a negative impact on the business, the lender must remove the assets from the online loan application, obtain an updated verification report that excludes the business account, and resubmit the loan to DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When retirement assets are entered in DU, DU issues a message requiring the lender to ensure that withdrawals are permitted, and that withdrawals are not limited to those completed in connection with the borrower's employment termination, retirement, or death. If any of these conditions are present, the lender must remove the retirement account assets from the online loan application, obtain an updated verification report that excludes the retirement account, and resubmit the loan to DU.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: If the lender would like to use the asset verification report to supplement the credit risk assessment in DU, see Rent Payment History in B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU  for additional information.
